title: Fitting draught strip to sash windows
subtitle: Pile strip on the sashes, foam on the meeting rail
excerpt: Sash windows draught through the parting bead channels, the meeting rail, and the pulley holes. Pile strip in the channels and self-adhesive foam on the meeting rail halve the infiltration. The job takes two to three hours per window and costs .
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust sash window draught-proofing guidance. Historic England guidance on sash window repair (historicengland.org.uk), compatible with conservation principles when using pile strip rather than foam.

content.text: A sash window has three draught paths. Most air comes through the parting bead channels where the sashes slide. The meeting rail in the middle, where the two sashes overlap, leaks more when the sashes are loose. The pulley covers at the top of the box leak too. Most draughts can be cut by 80% without touching the box frame.

The correct product for the parting bead channels is pile strip (also called brush strip or mohair strip), a polypropylene or nylon brush mounted in an aluminium or plastic carrier. Pile strip allows the sash to slide while filling the gap. Foam strip compresses and tears when the sash moves; do not use foam in the channels.
Pile strip (brush strip), 5-9 mm pile height, for the four channel runs. Measure the sash height and buy double (one run each side, both sashes).
Self-adhesive foam strip, P-profile, for the meeting rail and top of upper sash.
Tape measure and sharp utility knife or scissors.
Slide the bottom sash fully up and the top sash fully down, exposing both channels. Clean dust and old paint flakes from the channel faces with a dry cloth.

Cut four lengths of pile strip to the full sash height. Peel the adhesive backing and press each strip into the channel, with the pile facing the sash edge. One strip on each side of each sash run, four strips total.
Step 3, check the sashes still slide.

Return both sashes to the closed position and slide each one fully in both directions. The sashes should move with slight resistance from the pile but without sticking. If a sash sticks, the pile strip is too thick for the channel; replace with a thinner pile strip.
Wipe the top face of the bottom sash's meeting rail clean. Close both sashes.

Press P-profile foam along the top face of the bottom sash meeting rail, so it compresses against the bottom face of the top sash when both sashes are closed. The foam should compress to about half depth with both sashes shut; if it prevents the sashes closing flush, it is too thick.

items.fix: Use a thinner pile height (5 mm instead of 9 mm) or plane the channel face back 1-2 mm.
items.cause: Strip too thick for the channel
items.symptom: Pile strip stops sash from sliding

items.fix: Fit a short length of foam strip along the top rail of the upper sash where it meets the head of the box frame.
items.cause: Pulley pocket or top rail gap not sealed
items.symptom: Draught still present at top of upper sash
